Acoustic Damper Placement and
Tuning for Annular Combustors:
An Adjoint-Based Optimization
Study
Thermoacoustic instabilities pose a major threat to modern gas turbines. The use of
acoustic dampers, like Helmholtz resonators, has proven useful for the mitigation of such
instabilities. However, assessing the effect of acoustic dampers on thermoacoustic modes
in annular combustion chambers remains an intricate task. This results from the implicit
nature of the thermoacoustic Helmholtz equation associated with the high number of pos-
sible parameter values for the positioning of the dampers and their impedance design. In
the present work, the principal challenges of the effective placement and the design of the
impedance of acoustic dampers in annular chambers are discussed. This includes the
choice of an appropriate objective function for the optimization, the combinatorial chal-
lenges when dealing with different possible damper arrangements, and the numerical
complexities when using the thermoacoustic Helmholtz equation to approach this issue.
As a key aspect, the paper proposes a new adjoint-based approach to tackle these prob-
lems. The new algorithm establishes algebraic models that predict the effect of acoustic
dampers on the growth rates of the thermoacoustic modes. The theory is exempliﬁed on
the basis of a generic annular combustor model with 12 burners.
[DOI: 10.1115/1.4035201]

Introduction
Thermoacoustic instabilities pose a major threat to modern gas
turbines. These instabilities have negative effects on the opera-
tional range of the combustors and may severely damage the
machines [1]. Both passive and active damping methods may be
considered to tackle this issue. Usually, passive methods are used
because they feature high reliability at low costs. Such methods
are based on the use of acoustic damping devices like Helmholtz
dampers. It is very difﬁcult to adapt these components after
machine installation. Thus, the availability of fast and accurate
mathematical design tools is essential for the development of new
gas turbines featuring a higher efﬁciency and greater lifetime.
Large eddy simulations may be considered for the assessment
of thermoacoustic instabilities [2–4], but their numerical costs are
too high to be used for design purposes. As an alternative, the
governing equations can be linearized and transformed to the fre-
quency domain. Thermoacoustic instabilities can then be modeled
using a thermoacoustic Helmholtz equation:
r  ðc2r^pÞ þ x2^p ¼ ixðc  1Þ^q
(1)
Here, c denotes the local speed of sound, ^p is the Fourier trans-
form of the pressure ﬂuctuation; x is the complex frequency of
oscillation, c is the ratio of speciﬁc heats, and ^q is the Fourier
transform of the volumetric unsteady heat release rate. To close
the problem, the heat release rate can be related to the velocity
ﬂuctuation at a reference point by means of a ﬂame transfer func-
tion. Finally, this allows the heat release rate to be related to the
acoustic ﬁeld by a linear differential operator Q.
ixðc  1Þ^q ¼ Q^p
(2)
The linear operator Q evaluates the axial velocity ﬂuctuation at a
reference position as axial component of the gradient of the pres-
sure ﬂuctuation. It also accounts for a time lag and the spatial dis-
tribution of the heat release zone.
Finally, the Helmholtz equation takes the form of a nonlinear
eigenvalue problem with eigenvalue x and eigenfunction ^p. For
convenience, the problem can be written as

title: Institut f€ur Str€omungsmechanik und
text:
method to overcome this issue. Dowling and Stow used this tech-
nique to study a generic geometry and derive rules for the best
positioning of acoustic dampers [7]. Bothien et al. combined net-
work models with a genetic optimization algorithm to optimize a
new damper design [8]. Recently, Schuermans et al. used the
same approach for the design of a novel damping–cooling system
for Alstom’s GT26/GT24 engines [9]. However, the numerical
efﬁciency of the network models arises from a signiﬁcant geomet-
rical reduction of the originally three-dimensional geometry to a
network of one-dimensional surrogate components, if the model is
not coupled to a spatial discretization method like ﬁnite elements.
Full domain discretization methods may therefore be consid-
ered, in order to maintain the geometrical complexity of the actual
system in the model representation. Camporeale et al. used a ﬁnite
element method (FEM) to study the effect of multiple Helmholtz
resonators equipped to an annular combustion chamber [6]. They
observed that the application of Helmholtz dampers splits the res-
onant mode into two modes with distinct frequencies. This can be
attributed to the loss of discrete rotational symmetry. However,
this approach is numerically too complex to be used as fast design
tool.
Adjoint perturbation theory offers an opportunity to overcome
this problem. It approximates the change in the eigenfrequency
and the eigenfunction of nonlinear, nonhermitian eigenvalue prob-
lems due to changes in the involved operators. The following
problem is considered:
If x0 and ^p0 is a known eigenpair of the operator family L0,
how does it change when the family is altered to

to facilitate the approximation. This results in explicit expressions

faster to evaluate than the implicit eigenvalue problem.

perturbation theory can be utilized to (i) ﬁnd the best feasible
positioning pattern for a set of acoustic dampers and (ii) perform a
local optimization within the space of damper parameters. The

The discrete rotational symmetry of annular combustors is a
trigger of two-fold mode degeneracy for most of its thermoacous-
tic modes. The application of dampers may break this symmetry
and lead to two modes oscillating at different eigenfrequencies
close to the original degenerate frequency [7,15]. This makes the
correct computation of the mode unfolding highly relevant for the
current context.
The Role of Epsilon
Some authors consider the perturbation parameter e as rather
conceptual. It is therefore set to one after the derivation of the
equations. However, it can be linked to physical parameters [10].
In this paper, these parameters are the design parameters f for the
acoustic dampers. This offers the opportunity to interpret the
power series ans€atze (7) and (8) as functions describing the inﬂu-
ence of the physical parameters on the mode shape and frequency.
The accuracy of these functions is limited due to their asymptotic
nature and hence a method for the estimation of the approximation
error is needed. This error g is deﬁned as deviation of the correct
eigenvalue from the eigenvalue prediction:

A common approach for the estimation of this error would be
assuming that it is of the same order as the ﬁrst neglected term in
the series. There is no proof that this truncation rule applies in the
current context, but various examples from the ﬁeld of quantum
mechanics involving the Schr€odinger equation, which is a wave
equation as well, are known, see, e.g., Ref. [10]. Moreover, there
is a variety of mathematical and scientiﬁc examples for perturba-
tions where the truncation rule applies [16]. We therefore set up a
numerical study investigating a Rijke tube with a variable imped-
ance at the outlet. This study will demonstrate that the truncation
rule applies for the investigation of thermoacoustic modes and
hence facilitate a fast estimation of the perturbation error. In this
study, a simple two-dimensional Rijke tube was considered. An n-
s-model was used to model the ﬂame response. The cold gas tem-
perature was set to Tu ¼ 300 K while the hot gas temperature was
set to Tb ¼ 1500 K. The ratio of speciﬁc heats was set to c ¼ 1:4.
The geometry is depicted in Fig. 1. The interaction index was set
to n ¼ 0.1 and the time delay to s ¼ 2 ms. The reference point for
the ﬂame transfer function was located in the middle of the tube
0.01 m upstream of the ﬂame.

elements was employed to discretize the thermoacoustic Helm-

The discrete rotational symmetry of annular combustors is a
trigger of two-fold mode degeneracy for most of its thermoacous-
tic modes. The application of dampers may break this symmetry
and lead to two modes oscillating at different eigenfrequencies
close to the original degenerate frequency [7,15]. This makes the
correct computation of the mode unfolding highly relevant for the
current context.

Some authors consider the perturbation parameter e as rather
conceptual. It is therefore set to one after the derivation of the
equations. However, it can be linked to physical parameters [10].

is a common feature of perturbation theory. It is known as

Please note the slightly increasing offset between the ﬁrst
neglected term and the error for increasing perturbation strengths.
For the current case, the magnitude of the ﬁrst neglected term is
larger than that of the error when machine precision is not limiting
the calculation. Further considerations have to clarify whether this
is a general feature or cases might exist where the ﬁrst neglected
term slightly underestimates the error. No matter whether the
deviation overrates the error or not, it will scale with the perturba-
tion strength. Hence, the offset increases with the perturbation
strength. Thus, the method may fail to predict the error when the
perturbation strength becomes very large. However, because the
the error is also very large this is irrelevant for practical purposes.
The results indicate that the truncation rule holds true for the
considered test case. This supports the hypothesis that the trunca-
tion rule applies for perturbations of the thermoacoustic Helmholtz
equation. The results also emphasize that the error decreases with
the perturbation strength. The truncation rule can be utilized to cal-
culate the strength of a parameter shift such that the error is not
greater than a speciﬁed tolerance. This is a crucial aspect in view of
the possible divergence of the applied power series. Most impor-
tantly, it allows to limit the perturbation strength such that the pre-
dicted eigenvalue shift is highly accurate, a key idea for the
iterative optimization algorithm proposed later.

a multiparameter perturbation theory is necessary, i.e., a mathe-
matical tool to approximate solutions of the following perturba-
tion problem:

The Optimization Algorithm
Multiparameter perturbation theory provides the key ingredient
for an optimization algorithm to position and tune acoustic damp-
ers. Therefore, the problem can be split into two major steps,
namely positioning and tuning of the dampers. An illustration of
the scheme is displayed in Fig. 3.
First, the best positioning is computed. The optimization starts
with one classical iterative solution of the baseline conﬁguration.
Solutions of the Helmholtz equation are the computationally most
expensive operations in this algorithm. Evaluations of the explicit
multiparameter sensitivity functions (22) are much faster. Thus,
assessing all considered positioning patterns with respect to an
objective function f in a brute force sensitivity study is feasible
when using the base-line solution as input for these asymptotic
computations. The value of the objective function is a quality
measure of the desired design goals. The function should assess
the damping rate of the considered modes and can contain penalty
terms accounting for design constraints. Because the argument of
the objective function can be expressed as a direct function chain
starting with the design parameters fp, continuing with the pertur-
bation parameters ep, and ending with eigenvalues xðmÞ of the con-
sidered modes it can be explicitly written as
f ¼ fðfÞ
(33)
The pattern with the best impact to the objective function can then
be chosen. It deﬁnes a parameter change Dfbestpos.
In the second step, ﬁne tuning of the applied dampers is con-
ducted. The parameter perturbation L0 yielding the best possible
local improvement with respect to the objective function f can be
found at ﬁrst order using the multiparameter theory. To achieve
this, again only one expensive solution of the Helmholtz equation
is required. The results from this solution provide the basis for the
perturbative optimization. Therefore, the problem must be formu-
lated as superposition of the individual parameter perturbations

using the optimization algorithm. This is especially necessary to
deﬁne directions in the parameter space. The applied scaling con-
siderably affects the convergence of the algorithm. Figure 4 illus-
trates the problem for a two-dimensional parameter space.

Of course, the chosen step size must also account for other param-
eter constraints, e.g., lower and upper parameter bounds. Iterating
this procedure leads to successive improvements of the parame-
ters. This scheme can therefore be characterized as iterative local
optimization based on directional search with error-related step
size adaption.
Scaling the Parameter Space
The parameters determining the impedance have different phys-
ical units. Therefore, an appropriate scaling must be applied when
using the optimization algorithm. This is especially necessary to
deﬁne directions in the parameter space. The applied scaling con-
siderably affects the convergence of the algorithm. Figure 4 illus-
trates the problem for a two-dimensional parameter space.
The cyan lines represent contour lines of an objective function.
The best possible point is to be found in the upper right corner.
When deﬁning directions as vectors pointing on the unit disk (unit
hyper-sphere for higher dimensions), the applied scaling affects
the shape of this disk and therefore the determination of the best
possible direction. In particular, the disk may be distorted to an
ellipse and the longer semi-axis leads to a possibly overr

Application to a Generic Annular Combustor Model
In order to test the algorithm, a two-dimensional annular com-
bustor model was set up. The model is a simpliﬁcation of the
geometry proposed by Evesque and Polifke [18]. It neglects the
radial dimension of the chamber. The geometry and the mesh
used for the computations are depicted in Fig. 5.
It features twelve burner tubes connecting a plenum with the
actual combustion chamber. Each ﬂame response is modeled in
terms of an n-s-model. The cold gas temperature was set to
Tu ¼ 300 K and the hot gas temperature to Tb ¼ 1500 K. The inter-
action index was set to n ¼ 0.1 and the time delay to s ¼ 3 ms. The
reference points were located on the center axis of the burner
tubes 0.04 m downstream of the burner outlet. Variable impedance
boundaries were deﬁned to the left and right of each burner outlet.
The ratio of speciﬁc heats was set to c ¼ 1:4. In total, this allowed
to specify 24 independent impedances. For simplicity, a three
parameter model was used to model the reduced impedances:

The acoustic properties of the chamber thus depend on 72
parameters.
The impedance model was proposed by Nicoud et al. in favor of
its easy integration into an FEM Helmholtz solver [5]. Its physical
interpretation is therefore limited. We use it to test the algorithm. A
beneﬁt of the chosen impedance model is that the perturbation
operators are linear with respect to the change of the model param-
eters. Thus, the parameter changes Df can be directly used as per-
turbation parameters. However, physical impedance models can be
described as rational function with analytic nominator and denomi-
nator [19]. If such models are used, a linearization has to be applied
in order to obtain the perturbation parameters.

text:
Conclusions and Future Work
An adjoint-based method for the optimum positioning and tun-
ing of multiple acoustic dampers has been derived. As a key
aspect, the method utilizes the truncation rule. Application to a
model problem indicates that this rule is applicable to solutions of
the thermoacoustic Helmholtz equation. The method also rests on
a multiparameter formulation of the ﬁrst-order eigenvalue pertur-
bations. Even if not shown in this paper, it is possible to combine
the method with the Bloch wave methodology to exploit the dis-
crete rotational symmetry of the system in order to further acceler-
ate the computations [21]. To account for physical impedance
models, linearization of the involved perturbation operators will
be necessary. This together with a three-dimensional combustor
model will be the topic of future work in order to validate the
approach for real combustors.
The optimization algorithm could also be used to optimize
other entities than acoustic dampers. For instance, it could be uti-
lized to ﬁnd the best fuel staging pattern if the effects are available
as ﬂame response functions. Moreover, the multiparameter formu-
lation could help calculating uncertainty quantiﬁcations like those
discussed in Ref. [22]. The main limitations of the framework
arise from the fact that the multiparameter perturbation is of ﬁrst
order. Thus, a higher-order perturbation formulation would
improve the methodology. For instance, it would allow for global
optimization and larger step sizes. Feeding the results from the
tuning step back into the positioning step may also lead to
improvements of the algorithm. A general application for the pre-
sented perturbation theory could also be the acceleration of the
iterative algorithms for the solution of the Helmholtz equation.
Future work will address these topics.
Acknowledgment
Our thanks go to Philip E. Buschmann for his support while
programming the FEM code and his help with the graphics
preparation.
